transcript: search of El Dorado. But they all failed. Most ended in starvation, disease, and death. No one found a golden city, but they did record signs that one could exist. Spanish explorers claimed to see cities that glistened in white, great quantities of maize, a town of disproportionate size, and a chief with gold idols and very large towns to such an extent that they were astounded. Explorations continued over the next 200 years or so, but by the 19th century the legend of El Dorado had been dismissed as a myth. It wasn't until the early 20th century that these reports inspired a British explorer to revive the search. For two decades Fawcett scoured the Amazon for remnants of an ancient city, focusing on two areas, the Western Amazon in Bolivia and the Southern Amazon in Brazil. But he couldn't find any of the things the early European explorers observed. He met indigenous people but described them in his letters as living in small groups.
